metadata.summary: Evidence-based process for deciding whether a problem is worth solving: collect evidence across a validation hierarchy (paid > committed > engaged > stated > anecdote), apply Mom Test question rewrites to avoid leading/hypothetical framing, and output a PROCEED / PIVOT / KILL decision with a scored evidence log.

content.title: Problem Validation
content.summary: Before committing engineering resources to a solution, collect evidence across a validation hierarchy (paid > committed > engaged > stated > anecdote), apply Mom Test question rewrites to avoid leading/hypothetical framing, and output a PROCEED / PIVOT / KILL decision with a scored evidence log. Define a kill threshold before collecting any evidence.

why.p: Building products for unvalidated problems is the leading cause of startup failure. The validation hierarchy forces the researcher to distinguish between weak evidence (someone said they have the problem) and strong evidence (someone pre-paid to solve it). Without a pre-defined kill threshold, researchers rationalize weak evidence as sufficient and continue building.

list.item: Before committing engineering resources to a solution
After pain point research has identified candidate problems — which are worth solving?
Quarterly review of assumed problems in an existing product backlog
Pre-pivot: does the proposed direction have enough validated demand?
When a founder or PM has a strong hypothesis and needs structured pushback
When real users have already been paying for the solution — validation complete, move to retention
As a substitute for usability testing (problem validation answers "does this problem exist?", not "does this UI work?")
When n < 5 data points — a single frustrated user is anecdote, not signal
When all evidence is compliments and hypotheticals — that is negative validation, stop and pivot

rule.statement: Rank evidence by tier before synthesizing: 1-paid (strongest), 2-committed (LOI/pre-order), 3-engaged (prototype use, 3+ return visits), 4-stated (expressed interest in interview), 5-anecdote (single mention). Only tiers 1-3 justify PROCEED.
rule.rationale: Stated interest is insufficient for a build decision; people are optimistic about their future selves. Only behavioral evidence (paid, committed, engaged) predicts actual adoption.

rule.statement: Replace hypothetical and leading questions with past-behavior and present-reality questions: "Would you use this?" becomes "How do you currently solve X?"; "Is this a problem?" becomes "Tell me about the last time you dealt with X"; "Would you pay?" becomes "What have you tried? What did it cost?"
rule.rationale: Hypothetical questions produce socially desirable answers. Past-behavior questions produce facts that can be verified and compared across interviewees.

example.note: "Would you use this app?" — hypothetical, primes a yes response regardless of actual intent.
"Walk me through how you handle [task] today. What did you try last time it went wrong?" — anchors to past behavior, not future speculation.

rule.statement: After each validation interview, classify evidence by signal type: Time (scheduled follow-up, unprompted prototype use), Reputation (offered to introduce to colleague or boss), Money (pre-order, LOI, payment before product exists). Absence of all three = stated interest only.
rule.rationale: Commitment signals require the user to give up something real. Stated interest costs them nothing and therefore predicts nothing.

rule.statement: Treat these responses as negative validation signals: compliments ("great idea!"), hypotheticals ("I would definitely..."), generic statements ("everyone needs this"), approval-fishing responses. If an interview produces only these, the hypothesis is not confirmed.
rule.rationale: Polite users optimize for the researcher's comfort, not for truth. Compliments and hypotheticals are the mode, not the exception, in poorly structured validation interviews.

rule.statement: Run validation in five steps: (1) state the problem hypothesis; (2) define the kill threshold before collecting any evidence; (3) collect evidence from 10+ data points across multiple source types; (4) label each piece of evidence with its hierarchy tier; (5) decide PROCEED, PIVOT (with direction), or KILL.
rule.rationale: Defining the kill threshold first prevents post-hoc rationalization of weak evidence. The hierarchy labeling step forces the researcher to confront whether evidence is behavioral or just stated.

example.note: Kill threshold defined upfront: "If fewer than 3 out of 10 interviewees confirm the pain at intensity 7+/10, we stop." After 10 interviews: 7/10 confirmed, avg intensity 8.2. Decision: PROCEED to solution validation.

rule.statement: Distinguish problem frequency (how often it occurs) from problem intensity (how much it hurts); both must exceed the threshold for a build decision.
rule.rationale: A daily-but-minor problem and a rare-but-catastrophic problem require different solutions; conflating them produces a product that addresses neither adequately.

rule.statement: A competitor existing is weak evidence (someone thinks the problem is real); a competitor churning customers is strong evidence (the problem persists unsolved).
rule.rationale: Markets with active competitors validate demand but not opportunity; churn data from those competitors validates that the problem remains unsolved at scale.

rule.statement: LLMs cannot interview real users, cannot assess commitment signals from text alone, and will exhibit availability bias toward problems common in training data; use agents only to design the validation protocol and synthesize collected evidence.
rule.rationale: LLMs trained on public text overrepresent developer pain and underrepresent niche B2B pain; agent-generated PROCEED recommendations based on stated interest must be treated as PIVOT until behavioral evidence is collected.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: An agent is useful for designing the validation protocol (which signals to collect, from which sources, and how to interpret them) and for synthesizing collected evidence against the validation hierarchy. The agent cannot collect evidence itself without tool access, but given a brief summary of collected interviews, forum posts, and behavioral signals, it can score evidence quality and recommend proceed/pivot/kill. A lightweight human-in-loop checkpoint is required after every evidence collection phase.

template.purpose: Problem Validation Report with evidence table and PROCEED/PIVOT/KILL decision
LLM prompt to score evidence and recommend proceed/pivot/kill
LLM prompt to rewrite questions using Mom Test principles
Score evidence items by hierarchy level and print validation percentage
